14: YHWH yireh asher yeamer hayom behar YHWH yeraeh (Yhwh yirʾeh ... ʾăšer yēʾāmēr hayyôm bĕhar Yhwh yērāʾeh): 'YHWH will provide / YHWH will be seen.' The name YHWH-Yireh (YHWH will see/provide) is a word-play: raah means both 'see' and 'provide' (to see to it = to provide). The place-name combines both meanings: on this mountain YHWH sees and is seen. The site is identified with Jerusalem and eventually Mount Moriah (2 Chr 3:1), where Solomon built the temple. The Aqedah's theology: the provision of the ram in place of Isaac establishes the substitution-pattern that the entire sacrificial system develops and that Christ fulfills finally.
1: וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (wayhî ʾaḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh) — "after these things." The narrative connective following the treaty and Beersheba period. וְהָאֱלֹהִים נִסָּה אֶת-אַבְרָהָם (wĕhāʾĔlōhîm nissāh ʾet-ʾaḇrāhām) — "and God tested Abraham." Nissāh (Piel of nāsāh, to test, try, prove) — the narrator reveals the test's nature immediately, so the reader knows what Abraham does not. The same verb is used for the wilderness testing of Israel (Exod 15:25; 16:4; 20:20; Deut 8:2, 16). Testing (nissāyôn) in the OT is not divine cruelty but divine disclosure: the test reveals what is already in the heart. Hinnēnî (here I am, ready, present) — Abraham's response; the formula of radical availability to God's call (used also in Exod 3:4; Isa 6:8).
2: קַח-נָא אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידְךָ אֲשֶׁר-אָהַבְתָּ אֶת-יִצְחָק — "take now your son, your only son, whom you love, Isaac." The five-fold specification is deliberately cumulative: (1) bĕnḵā (your son); (2) yĕḥîḏĕḵā (your only son — from yāḥîḏ, only, unique, alone); (3) ʾăšer-ʾāhaḇtā (whom you love — the first use of the verb ʾāhab, love, in the OT); (4) ʾet-yiṣḥāq (Isaac — named last, after the emotional buildup). John 3:16 echoes this structure: "his one and only Son" (monogenē) and "loved" (ēgápēsen). לֶךְ-לְךָ — the second great lēḵ-lĕḵā command, matching 12:1; the two commands bracket the Abraham narrative.
3: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר — "Abraham rose early in the morning." No recorded hesitation, no dialogue with Sarah, no delay — the narrative's silence on the night between the command and the morning is as significant as any speech could be. Wayyaḥăbōš ʾet-ḥămōrô (he saddled his donkey) — the patriarch himself saddles the animal, not his servants; the personal involvement signals the seriousness and directness of the response. וַיְבַקַּע עֲצֵי עֹלָה (he cut the wood for the burnt offering) — the preparatory work is complete before the journey begins; Abraham takes the wood, the fire (ʾēš), and the knife (maʾăḵelet, cleaver, knife for slaughter).
4: בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי (bayyôm haššĕlîšî, on the third day) — "the third day" in the OT carries the weight of divine action following a period of apparent inactivity: deliverance at the Red Sea (Exod 14-15 measured from departure), resurrection (Hos 6:2; Jon 1:17; 1 Cor 15:4), the giving of the Torah (Exod 19:11, 16). Hebrews 11:19 explicitly reads the Akedah through the resurrection lens: Abraham "considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead, from which, figuratively speaking, he did receive him back." The three-day journey places the Akedah in the theological register of death-and-restoration. וַיַּשָּׂא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֵינָיו — "Abraham lifted up his eyes" — the seeing-command formula of 13:14 now fulfilled by Abraham's own initiative.
5: שְׁבוּ-לָכֶם פֹּה עִם-הַחֲמוֹר — "stay here with the donkey." The two servants are left behind; father and son go alone. אֲנִי וְהַנַּעַר נֵלְכָה עַד-כֹּה (ʾănî wĕhannāʿar nēlĕḵāh ʿaḏ-kōh) — "I and the boy will go over there." וְנִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה וְנָשׁוּבָה אֲלֵיכֶם (wĕništaḥăweh wĕnāšûḇāh ʾălêḵem) — "we will worship and we will return to you." The first-person plural "we" is either (1) Abraham's faith statement: he believed both of them would return (Heb 11:19); or (2) a social euphemism to prevent the servants from interfering. The grammar permits both readings; the theology of Heb 11:19 takes the first seriously.
6: וַיַּשֶׂם אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֲצֵי הָעֹלָה עַל-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ — "Abraham placed the wood of the burnt offering on his son Isaac." The wood is placed on the son — a detail noted in patristic reading as a type of the cross-bearing (John 19:17: Jesus "carrying his own cross"). Whether intended by the narrator or not, the structural parallel is precise: the son carries the instrument of his own sacrifice. וַיִּקַּח בְּיָדוֹ אֶת-הָאֵשׁ וְאֶת-הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת (he took in his hand the fire and the knife) — Abraham carries the dangerous items himself, protecting Isaac from the fire and the knife.
7: הִנֶּה הָאֵשׁ וְהָעֵצִים וְאַיֵּה הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה — "the fire and the wood are here, but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?" Isaac's question is innocent and devastating: he sees the preparations, notes the missing element, asks. Śeh (lamb, sheep/goat for offering) — the specific sacrificial animal vocabulary. The question hangs in the air as the central tension of the narrative: where is the offering? The answer of v. 8 is at once evasive and prophetic.
8: אֱלֹהִים יִרְאֶה-לּוֹ הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה בְּנִי — "God will provide for himself the lamb for the burnt offering, my son." Yirʾeh-llô — the reflexive dative (lô, for himself) with the verb rāʾāh (to see): "God will see/provide for himself." This is the etymological basis of the name YHWH-Yireh in v. 14 (YHWH yirʾeh): where God sees, God provides. The expression is ambiguous in the moment — Abraham may be speaking a genuine theological principle without knowing how it will apply; the narrative reveals that the principle was literally and immediately true. וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם יַחְדָּו (the two of them went together) — the phrase is repeated from v. 6 and will occur again after Isaac's question: "together" (yaḥdāw) at every stage, despite the terrible knowledge Abraham carries.
9: וַיַּעֲקֹד אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ (wayyaʿăqōḏ ʾet-yiṣḥāq bĕnô) — "he bound his son Isaac." ʿĀqaḏ (to bind, tie — specifically the binding of feet or limbs; this verb appears only here in the OT) — the Akedah takes its name from this word. The binding of Isaac is the technical term for the preparatory restraint of a sacrifice before slaughter. וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתוֹ עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ מִמַּעַל לָעֵצִים (he laid him on the altar, on top of the wood) — the altar, the wood, the bound son: the preparation is complete. There is no recorded resistance from Isaac, no recorded cry from Abraham.
10: וַיִּשְׁלַח אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יָדוֹ וַיִּקַּח אֶת-הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת לִשְׁחֹט אֶת-בְּנוֹ — "Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slaughter his son." The verb šāḥaṭ (to slaughter, kill in the ritual sense used for sacrificial animals — cf. Lev 1:5; 3:2, 8, 13) confirms that the act Abraham was about to perform was not murder but sacrifice: he was treating his son as a sacrificial animal, as God had commanded. The hand is stretched out (šālaḥ yāḏ) — the same phrase as when God stretched out his hand in judgment (Exod 7:19; 9:15).
11: וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה מִן-הַשָּׁמַיִם — "the angel of the LORD called to him from heaven." The intervention is from heaven — divine authority from above; in ch. 16 and 21 the angel appeared in the desert; here the heavenly call matches the elevated location (the mountain) and the elevation of the stakes. אַבְרָהָם אַבְרָהָם — the doubled name is the divine address of urgent importance: Moses! Moses! (Exod 3:4); Samuel! Samuel! (1 Sam 3:10); Martha, Martha! (Luke 10:41); Saul, Saul! (Acts 9:4). The repetition halts the action at the critical moment. Hinnēnî — "here I am" — the same response as v. 1; Abraham remains in readiness even at the moment of the knife.
12: כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי-יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה (kî ʿattāh yāḏaʿtî kî-yĕrēʾ ʾĔlōhîm ʾattāh) — "for now I know that you are a God-fearer." Yāḏaʿtî (I have come to know, I know) — the divine statement of knowing after the test does not imply prior ignorance; it is a forensic declaration: the test has produced observable evidence that the claim of faith is real. לֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידְךָ מִמֶּנִּי — "you have not withheld your son, your only son, from me." Ḥāśaḵ (to withhold, spare, hold back) — Paul cites the parallel in Rom 8:32: "He who did not spare (ouk epheisato) his own Son but gave him up for us all" — the language of the Akedah is applied to the Father's giving of the Son.
13: אַיִל אַחַר נֶאֱחַז בַּסְּבַךְ בְּקַרְנָיו — "a ram caught in a thicket by its horns." ʾAyil (ram, adult male sheep — specifically the animal used in burnt offerings, Lev 8:18-21; Num 29:2) — the animal is a ram, not the śeh (lamb) that Isaac asked about. The ram appears caught — God's provision arrives already prepared, already there; the ram was caught in the thicket while Abraham was raising the knife. Wayyiqaḥ ʾet-hāʾayil wayyaʿălēhû lĕʿōlāh taḥat bĕnô (he took the ram and offered it as a burnt offering instead of his son) — taḥat (instead of, in place of, as a substitute for) — substitutionary sacrifice: the ram dies in the place of the son.
15: וַיִּקְרָא מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה אֶל-אַבְרָהָם שֵׁנִית מִן-הַשָּׁמָיִם — "the angel of the LORD called to Abraham a second time from heaven." The second heavenly word (vv. 16-18) is the divine oath — the highest level of divine commitment possible. The first call (vv. 11-12) halted the sacrifice; the second call confirms the covenant in the most solemn form. The two calls frame the Akedah's resolution: rescue (first call) and covenant renewal (second call).
16: בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי (bî nišbaʿtî, by myself I have sworn) — the divine self-oath. God swears by himself because there is nothing greater to swear by — this is the theological argument of Heb 6:13-17: "since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself." The self-oath (bî nišbaʿtî = literally "by me I have sworn") elevates the covenant to its highest possible register. יַעַן אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה וְלֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידֶךָ — "because you have done this and have not withheld your son, your only son" — the covenant renewal is explicitly conditional on Abraham's obedience, but the oath makes the fulfillment unconditional once given.
17: כִּי-בָרֵךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ וְהַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ (kî-ḇārēḵ ʾăḇārĕḵĕḵā wĕharbbāh ʾarbbeh ʾet-zarʿĕḵā) — "I will surely bless you and greatly multiply your offspring." Two emphatic infinitive absolutes (bārēḵ ʾăḇārĕḵĕḵā; harbāh ʾarbbeh) — the most emphatic Hebrew grammar for absolute certainty. כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וְכַחוֹל אֲשֶׁר עַל-שְׂפַת הַיָּם — "as the stars of the sky and as the sand on the seashore" — both innumerability images combined in a single verse, restating and expanding 15:5 (stars) and 13:16 (dust). The offspring promise restated after the test with maximum rhetorical force. וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֲךָ אֵת שַׁעַר אֹיְבָיו — "your offspring will possess the gate of their enemies" — the gate (šaʿar) = the city; possessing the gate = conquering the city; the promise anticipates the conquest and establishment of the Davidic kingdom.
18: וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ — "all nations of the earth will be blessed through your offspring." Wĕhitbārăḵû — Hithpael (reflexive-passive: they will bless themselves through / they will be blessed through). The form here (Hithpael) creates ambiguity between reflexive ("all nations will bless themselves by your offspring") and passive ("all nations will be blessed through your offspring") — Gal 3:8 and Acts 3:25 quote this verse as passive (echoing 12:3 where the Niphal form appears). The promise ends with the most universal scope: kol gôyê hāʾāreṣ (all nations of the earth), reaching far beyond the land of Canaan to embrace the entire human community.
19: וַיָּשָׁב אַבְרָהָם אֶל-נְעָרָיו — "Abraham returned to his young men." The return is recorded briefly — no dialogue, no emotional commentary. The narrative lacunism is itself a feature: what passed between Abraham and Isaac on the descent is not told. Wayyāqom wayyēlĕḵû yaḥdāw ʾel-bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ (they rose and went together to Beersheba) — "together" (yaḥdāw) again: they went up together (v. 6), came down together (implied), and return together. The relationship survives the test intact.
20: The genealogical notice of Nahor's family (vv. 20-24) is positioned here for a narrative purpose: Rebekah appears as Bethuel's daughter (v. 23). The next major action in the Abraham narrative will be finding Isaac a wife (ch. 24), and this genealogy introduces Rebekah into the story before the betrothal narrative begins. The notice also signals that the covenant line (which narrowed through Isaac) will be continued by a wife from the family of Nahor — the broader Abraham family, not a Canaanite woman.
21: Nahor's eight sons through Milcah: עוּץ (ʿûṣ, Uz) is the firstborn — the land of Uz is Job's homeland (Job 1:1); בּוּז (bûz, Buz) gives his name to the Buzite clan (Job 32:2 — Elihu the Buzite). The Nahorite genealogy thus connects to the Job tradition: Job's world is the world of the broader Semitic family descended from Nahor. The connections between the patriarchal and wisdom literature traditions run through these genealogical notices.
22: Kesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel — these Nahorite names map onto the Aramean/northern Mesopotamian tribal geography. בְּתוּאֵל (Bĕtûʾēl, Bethuel) — "man of God" or possibly "daughter of God" (the name is unusual; some read it as "house of El") — the father of Rebekah and father-in-law of Isaac; he appears again in 24:15, 24, 47, 50; 25:20; 28:2, 5. The genealogy's descent to Bethuel is the narrative point of the list: it establishes Rebekah's family pedigree within the Nahorite-Semitic clan structure.
23: וּבְתוּאֵל יָלַד אֶת-רִבְקָה (ûḇĕtûʾēl yālaḏ ʾet-riḇqāh) — "Bethuel fathered Rebekah." Riḇqāh (Rebekah) — the name is introduced without commentary; she is "born" into the narrative at the moment the story needs her. The positioning of Rebekah's introduction immediately after the Akedah (where Isaac's existence is confirmed and the covenant line secured) and before the wife-search of ch. 24 is structurally elegant: the Akedah tests and secures Isaac; the genealogy introduces his future wife; ch. 24 brings them together.
24: וּפִילַגְשׁוֹ וּשְׁמָהּ רְאוּמָה (ûpîlaḡšô ûšĕmāh rĕʾûmāh) — "his concubine, whose name was Reumah." Pîleḡeš (concubine) — a secondary wife without the full legal status of the primary wife; a recognized category of marriage in the ANE. Nahor's four sons by Reumah complete his twelve sons total (eight through Milcah, four through Reumah) — a number that may deliberately parallel the twelve tribes of Israel who descend from Abraham's twelve-grandson structure (Ishmael also has twelve princes, 17:20; 25:12-16). The narrative geometry of twelve descendants is a recurring organizational principle.
